item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
An IHOP rewards card.

An IHOP rewards card.

An IHOP rewards card.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I dunno how I feel about this - as Riemann pointed out the "was-it-all-a-plan-or-am-I-just-a-master-manipulator" stuff is pretty obnoxious, and while I initially thought that the "literally anyone without a name or identification provided by a government is an instance of Nobody" was a bit of a laugh at the Foundation's expense in terms of only being able to interpret things through legible and unambiguous information, the twist that, "no, they're more or less right" didn't do much for me. This is a technically competent article, but not one that felt like it paid off the parts of this canon I have enjoyed. -1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I know Kirbs is gonna come after me for this downvote. But things like the "description" of the properties of 5877, the assumption "that any individual is an SCP-5877 instance until one of the following conditions are met", and editorialising like "observant nature" really shock me out of this one.

+ show block
– hide block

I get that it's tricky to define to define something by its negation, but as I prepare to learn what this is, I instead learn "oh we don't know anything about it." It's not phrased in the right way to actually convey the information - the only reason I actually know what it is is because this is a Nobody contest entry.

Since my suspension of disbelief has already broken, moving to the first interview, its placement in the broader context of the canon really does it a disservice. In the context of the article, we learn that all this cataclysm stuff is (possibly!) bullshit, which is a bit frustrating that our time was wasted but affords greater insight into the mind of this Nobody.

But - in the canon, this just kicks the can down the road further. What's the point of the Nobody/White rivalry? What are their goals? Who knows, and all that stuff we told you? Indeterminate amounts are nonsense, so don't even worry about it.

I think the attempt at the Usual Suspects twist would have been much more effective if there were more characters than Tyler. As it is, he just sort of talks through it himself and figures out the "I knew you wanted me to think that I wanted to read the book so I thought of that and said it before you!" by himself in a couple lines.

Finally, the choice to make the pseudo-Nobodies the anomaly do a real disservice to the article. They're almost immediately discarded their connection to Nobody and her interactions with them are cast into doubt in the small section that they're actually brought up again. Instead, we get talks of cataclysms and janitors and the Man in White - it makes me think that the team had an idea then realised they wanted to post an scp instead of a tale.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Ah, my bad. That's a holdover from a previous draft. Forgot to change the name. Good catch!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Amazing tale! This team's tales are keeping me hooked. One error though..

Maliz: Back to the handyman shit again?

Nobody: I'm sorry you're getting frustrated.

Holden: You don't mean that.

Nobody: I like you Tyler. I really do. You remind me of your mother. You keep your eye on as much as you can and you have a good grasp of what you don't know. But today, I need you to doubt. I need you to be my witness, but a careful one.

This was taken from an interview log. So, who is Holden?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Holy shit, another fantastic article for Nobody.

Two typos I caught:

"He oral reports indicate"

You keep you eye on as much as you can

Big +1, can't wait to see what's up next.

item.title: Re: Put A Name To The Face
Put A Name To The Face
item.content:encoded:
What's in a name? That which we call a rose by any other name would smell as sweet of tar and ichor.

Thanks to A Random Day, NamelessMediocre, and Oboegeek99 for critique.

Thanks to my team for nothing looking this over as well.

And thanks to you for reading!
